section_title: INTRODUCTION TO NWOG PART 2: FINDING REAL VALUE
content:
Welcome back to the second part of the ICT New Week Opening Gap (NWOG) closer look at this concept.
The core idea is finding real value in price action.

section_title: ICT LOGIC VS. RETAIL TRADING PERSPECTIVES
content:
When looking at price action, if you strip it back to retail logic for a moment, traders are usually trying to
buy support and trying to short resistance. My agenda has always been trying to get to the root cause or
the underpinnings of the marketplace. This involves looking for draws on liquidity areas that price should
support or resist based on logical reasoning, not some randomly selected horizontal or diagonal trend
support/resistance levels, which I am not a fan of.

section_title: NWOGS AS PRICE MAGNETS
content:
Each set of horizontal lines in the example represents an individual New Week Opening Gap. You can see
how price gravitates towards them. It may pass through them like a hot knife through butter, or it may reach
them, consolidate around or inside of them, and then expand to the next one.

section_title: NWOGS COMPARED TO PIVOT NUMBERS AND LIQUIDITY DRAWS
content:
If you've ever used pivot numbers (which used to be the floor trader's pivot numbers, and I used them
habitually before abandoning them for better tools), they acted as a magnet for price. New Week Opening
Gaps exhibit the same magnetic effect on price as a draw on liquidity, like equal highs or a single high
(when bullish) where price will invariably want to reach above old highs for buy stops, or reach down below
an old low or relatively equal lows for sell stops. That same magnetic effect is seen with New Week
Opening Gaps.

section_title: THE NON-RANDOM NATURE OF OPENING PRICES
content:
Opening prices are not random price events. The first opening trade, the first tick of the marketplace when
the new week opens at 6 PM New York local time, is absolutely not random. The proof of this is to watch
how price respects it throughout the week, and in weeks and months ahead in the future. It is important to
know the Sunday opening price and extend it through the entirety of the current trading week.

section_title: PROJECTING THE NWOG RANGE INTO THE FUTURE
content:
The entire New Week Opening Gap high and low range will be projected into the future for many weeks.
For example, the opening price on Sunday, May 7, 2023, will be extended throughout that trading week. To
define the opposite end of the New Week Opening Gap, we look at where price was on the previous Friday.
The difference between these two price points defines the New Week Opening Gap.

section_title: THE NON-RANDOM NATURE OF CLOSING PRICES AND NWOG RESPECT
content:
Much in the same vein that opening prices are not random price events, closing prices are not random.
While many in the industry claim prices are random and unpredictable, my students have observed how
price truly respects these New Week Opening Gaps, not just on the very day they form or that particular
week, but weeks and months in the future.

section_title: DEFINING THE ICT NEW WEEK OPENING GAP
content:
Let's take a closer look at the Friday, March 3rd, 2023, closing price, and the Sunday, March 5th, 2023,
opening price. The ICT New Week Opening Gap is the separation between these two price points.
- If Friday's closing price (March 3rd, 2023) is above the subsequent Sunday's opening price, then Friday's
closing price is the high of that New Week Opening Gap.
- The opening price on Sunday is the low of this particular gap.
- If the roles were reversed (if we opened above wherever we closed on Friday), then these labels would be
diametrically opposed.

section_title: EXTENDING NWOGS INTO FUTURE PRICE ACTION
content:
You can see the relationship when extending the March 3rd and March 5th New Week Opening Gap into
future price action. Price treats it as resistance, pulls away, rallies through, and comes back down. It's
important to notice that it respects the separation between these two price points. When buy side was
offered, what was lacking sell side? Movement on the downside. Price then moves away, runs buy side,
trades below the short-term low, and gravitates back and forth around this New Week Opening Gap, yet
still respecting it.

section_title: NWOGS AS FAIR VALUE FUNCTIONS AND MAGNETS
content:
Individual 5-minute candles show price finding support, springing higher for buy side trades up to an old
New Week Opening Gap, then trading back down to a New Week Opening Gap below. Price treats these
New Week Opening Gaps specifically as magnets; it creates this willingness to want to go right back to
them. It's a fair value function in price delivery.

section_title: THE ENGINEERED NATURE AND USE OF NWOGS BY SMART MONEY
content:
The Gap is not a random event; it's engineered and designed. Smart money uses these old reference
points throughout the future week of trading, the future month of trading, and for several months in the
future. The market finds some support, rallies away, consolidates around, and draws right back down into
that New Week Opening Gap once more.

section_title: TRENDING SIGNATURES WITH THE NEW WEEK OPENING GAP
content:
When a market indicates that it truly wants to run away and trend, there are many indicators. With the New
Week Opening Gap, if you see an aggressive, sudden move away from it, that indicates we are entering a
trending market. For instance, in the example with the March 5th, 2023 week, aggressive movement away
from the New Week Opening Gap means we are going to be trending significantly.

section_title: INTERPRETING NWOG MOVEMENT FOR TRENDING MODELS
content:
Looking at a larger fractal of that price action on the hourly chart, the movement away from the same New
Week Opening Gap shows significant price movement. This provides an 'x-ray view' behind price: if price
shows a willingness to sharply move away from the current New Week Opening Gap, it's likely that we're
entering a trending model. This means the market can move longer and with more magnitude behind the
price moves, instead of being a small, scalpers' consolidation market.

section_title: CONSOLIDATION SIGNATURES WITH NEW WEEK OPENING GAPS
content:
I look for a willingness to sharply move away, either above or below, a current New Week Opening Gap.
The 'current New Week Opening Gap' refers to the separation between Sunday's open and the previous
week's Friday close for the week currently being traded or the week to become. If price has an
unwillingness to move away and just keeps coming right back to that current New Week Opening Gap, then
we're probably going to be range-bound and consolidating, requiring a scalping approach with more nimble
trades and not expecting a trend.

section_title: MULTIPLE CONVERGING NWOGS INDICATE CONSOLIDATION
content:
Another characteristic of a consolidation market is when there's a multitude of New Week Opening Gaps
converging within a range. In the example, there's a New Week Opening Gap high, a New Week Opening
Gap low, another high, and another low, plus a shaded rectangle showing another NWOG. The
convergence of these three New Week Opening Gaps in close proximity for this particular week in history
indicates that the market will tend to enter a range-bounded environment.

section_title: TRADING STRATEGY IN CONVERGED NWOG ENVIRONMENTS
content:
When there is a convergence of multiple New Week Opening Gaps in close proximity, the markets will tend
to enter a range-bounded environment. This helps me as an analyst to point out in advance that we're likely
to be range-bound, not a big moving market, and thus requires a scalping approach. While there can still
be significant price moves, we're not looking for one-sidedness that lasts for days and weeks. Instead, we
anticipate highs and lows to be violated and then pulled back into the middle, with price continually drawing
back into or around these converged NWOGs.

section_title: KEEPING NWOGS ON CHARTS AND INTEGRATION WITH OTHER CONCEPTS
content:
It's important to keep these NWOGs on your chart. I suggest having a separate chart template labeled
'New Week Opening Gap' so you can constantly refer back to where we are in terms of these 'magnets' on
price action. By themselves, you don't just simply look at them and say 'price is going to go there.' You have
to incorporate other factors I've taught on this channel, in the core content, or in the 2022 mentorship
model. All these factors blended together give you a better understanding of where price is likely to draw to,
not just an old high or old low.

section_title: NWOGS AS MILE MARKERS AND ULTIMATE TARGETS
content:
Incorporate these old New Week Opening Gaps or the current New Week Opening Gap because price will
absolutely treat them as a magnet and go back to them, re-test them, and then many times spring away to
offer you a short-term scalp or maybe even a trending model. They can be mile markers (reaching for one
initially to see if it can reach the next one) or ultimate targets.
- In your notes, write down that if we have a New Week Opening Gap and price is likely to go higher, if it
can reach the halfway point between two NWOGs, the probabilities are that it will probably reach through
the next New Week Opening Gap in the series of the last several weeks and months of NWOGs that you
would keep on your chart.

section_title: NON-FARM PAYROLL EXAMPLE
content:
This example shows a current fractal in price action during the Friday Non-Farm Payroll on May 5th, 2023.
We saw price trade down below old lows where sell side liquidity would reside, and into an area considered
a fair value gap on the daily chart. The market then gyrates and gravitates around the New Week Opening
Gap from March 3rd. We are in May, using levels from March 3rd and March 5th (Friday's closing price and
Sunday's opening price after) extended throughout time, and look at the sensitivity here.

section_title: PRICE SENSITIVITY AND BEHAVIOR AROUND NWOGS
content:
Observe the closing price on that candle, the lower wick of that candle, and the bodies of the candles.
Notice how price respects it, with all the bodies stacking above the high of that old New Week Opening
Gap from March 3rd, 2023. Price then moves higher, comes back down, and after a Non-Farm Payroll
event, rallies and gravitates to the closest New Week Opening Gap, then reaches for the one above it. It
trades to that, comes back down, fills up a fair value gap, and expands away, reaching into the consequent
encroachment of a wick and revisiting an old imbalance.

section_title: DETERMINING THE NUMBER OF NWOGS TO USE (IPTA DATA RANGE)
content:
Looking at it closer on a 15-minute timeframe, with a fair value gap overlaid on the March 3rd, 2023, and
March 5th, 2023, New Week Opening Gap. You might wonder why this specific NWOG is used it happens
to be the one that runs through all this data. If you see price in consolidations or a cluster of price action,
go back through time and see what New Week Opening Gap extends through that area.
- The question then becomes: how many New Week Opening Gaps do I use in my charts? Whatever today
is in trading, I look back 60 days.

section_title: THE 60-DAY LOOKBACK AND IPTA DATA RANGE
content:
For those who have gone through my core content, in Month 5 (which is a lot of material), I teach the 'IPTA
data range' concept: a 60-day lookback and a 60-day cast forward. So, whatever today is, I look back 60
days, and whatever New Week Opening Gaps have formed in the last 60 days, that's how far I'll look back
and that's the maximum I have on my charts. For new, developing students, I counsel you to have at least
five, as that gives you the most salient ones right now.

section_title: ENDURING VALIDITY OF PD ARRAYS AND ALGORITHM PRICE DELIVERY
content:
As you improve and consider more information, you'll want to use that 60-day lookback period. Many paid
students initially discounted this 60-day concept, thinking it was of no concern, but with more information
and ongoing education, you can see its major impact on my analysis, including which PD array I consider
still valid.
- For instance, a Fair Value Gap 60 days old is still valid to me, even if it's already been 'filled in.' I am not
the average person who considers an imbalance 'done' once traded back up into. The algorithm is not done
with it; it's going to refer back to it and respect that same old level because of the time aspect incorporated
in algorithm price delivery.
